title: Interactive Python Programming
description: Learn Python programming through visual debugging and hands-on practice

modules.title: Programming Fundamentals
modules.description: Master the basics of Python programming including variables, data types, and simple operations

lessons.title: Your First Python Program
lessons.description: Learn how to write and run your very first Python program

steps.title: What is Programming?
steps.content:
Programming is like giving instructions to a computer. Just like you might give directions to a friend, you write code to tell the computer what to do. In Python, we write these instructions in a special language the computer understands.

Let's start with the most famous program in computer science: 'Hello, World!' This simple program teaches the computer to say hello to the world.

steps.title: Watch Your Code Run
steps.content: Now let's see what happens when we run our Hello World program. Watch carefully as each line of code executes step by step.

tooltips.content: This line defines a function called 'helloWorld'. Think of a function as a recipe - it contains instructions that we can use later.
The print() function tells Python to display text on the screen. The text inside the quotes is exactly what will appear.
Great! The program has finished running. The text 'Hello, World!' would appear on the screen.

steps.title: Test Your Understanding
steps.content: Let's see if you understand what this program does. Look at the code and predict what will happen.

quiz.question: What text will this program display when it runs?
quiz.explanation: Exactly right! The print() function displays whatever text is inside the quotes. In this case, it displays 'Hello, World!' on the screen.

steps.title: Make It Your Own
steps.content: Now it's your turn! Try changing the message inside the quotes to something personal. You could make it say your name, a favorite quote, or anything you like.
hints: You can put any text you want inside the quotes
Make sure to keep the quotes around your text
Click 'Run' to see your changes in action

lessons.title: Storing Information with Variables
lessons.description: Learn how to store and use information in your programs using variables
lessons.code:
def variablesDemo():
    name = 'Alice'
    age = 25
    print('Hello, my name is', name)
    print('I am', age, 'years old')

steps.title: What Are Variables?
steps.content:
Variables are like labeled boxes where you can store information. Just like you might put your toys in a box labeled 'toys', you can put data in a variable with a name.

In Python, you create a variable by giving it a name and storing something in it using the = sign. For example: name = 'Alice' creates a variable called 'name' and stores the text 'Alice' in it.

steps.title: Watch Variables in Action
steps.content: Let's see how variables work by watching them get created and used in our program.

tooltips.content: Here we create a variable called 'name' and store the text 'Alice' in it. Notice how the variable appears in the Variables panel!
Now we create another variable called 'age' and store the number 25 in it. Variables can hold different types of information!
Here we use the variables in our print statement. Python replaces 'name' with 'Alice' when the program runs.

steps.title: Predict the Output
steps.content: Look at the variables and predict what the program will print.

quiz.question: What will the first print statement display?

options.explanation: No, Python replaces the variable 'name' with its value.
Correct! Python uses the value stored in the variable 'name', which is 'Alice'.
No, that's the value of the 'age' variable, not 'name'.
No, this code will run without errors.

quiz.explanation: When you use a variable in Python, it gets replaced with whatever value is stored inside it.

steps.title: Create Your Own Variables
steps.content: Try creating your own variables! Change the name and age, or add new variables like favorite_color or hobby.
steps.editableCode:
def variablesDemo():
    name = 'Alice'
    age = 25
    print('Hello, my name is', name)
    print('I am', age, 'years old')
hints: Try changing 'Alice' to your own name
Change the age to your real age
Use your new variable in a print statement
Remember: text goes in quotes, numbers don't
